title: Processor Queue Length
spl:
index=perfmon sourcetype="Perfmon:System" counter="Processor Queue Length"
| timechart span=5m avg(Value) as queue_len by host
| where queue_len > 4
description: Processor queue length >2 per core indicates threads waiting for CPU time. Detects CPU contention even when average utilization looks normal due to burst patterns.
value: You catch CPU *contention* that averages hide, which is different from a simple high-percent CPU check.
implementation: Add `Processor Queue Length` to Perfmon System object inputs (interval=30). A sustained queue >2Ă— number of cores indicates saturation. Correlate with `Context Switches/sec` from the same object to distinguish CPU-bound workloads from excessive threading.
visualization: Line chart (queue trend), Heatmap (hosts Ă— time), Single value (current queue).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` avg(Performance.cpu_load_percent) as avg_cpu
  from datamodel=Performance where nodename=Performance.CPU
  by Performance.host span=5m
| where avg_cpu > 80
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
â€˘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_windows`.
â€˘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=Perfmon:System` (counter: Processor Queue Length).
â€˘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 â€” Configure data collection
Add `Processor Queue Length` to Perfmon System object inputs (interval=30). A sustained queue >2Ă— number of cores indicates saturation. Correlate with `Context Switches/sec` from the same object to distinguish CPU-bound workloads from excessive threading.

Step 2 â€” Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=perfmon sourcetype="Perfmon:System" counter="Processor Queue Length"
| timechart span=5m avg(Value) as queue_len by host
| where queue_len > 4
```

Understanding this SPL

**Processor Queue Length** â€” Processor queue length >2 per core indicates threads waiting for CPU time. Detects CPU contention even when average utilization looks normal due to burst patterns.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=Perfmon:System` (counter: Processor Queue Length). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_windows`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedâ€”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: perfmon; **sourcetype**: Perfmon:System. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

â€˘ Scopes the data: index=perfmon, sourcetype="Perfmon:System". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
â€˘ `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=5m** buckets with a separate series **by host** â€” ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.
â€˘ Filters the current rows with `where queue_len > 4` â€” typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` avg(Performance.cpu_load_percent) as avg_cpu
  from datamodel=Performance where nodename=Performance.CPU
  by Performance.host span=5m
| where avg_cpu > 80
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Processor Queue Length** â€” Processor queue length >2 per core indicates threads waiting for CPU time. Detects CPU contention even when average utilization looks normal due to burst patterns.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=Perfmon:System` (counter: Processor Queue Length). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_windows`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedâ€”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

â€˘ Uses `tstats` on the CIM data model in `cimModels` (see the accelerated SPL block). Enable that model in Data Model Acceleration.
â€˘ The `where` and `by` clauses mirror the intent of the primary SPL; if tstats is empty, confirm field aliases in Splunk CIM and the Windows TA.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 â€” Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 â€” Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (queue trend), Heatmap (hosts Ă— time), Single value (current queue).
grandmaExplanation: We help you know when a server has a line of work waiting for the processor, which can make everything feel slow even if average CPU is not redlining.
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate `Performance.CPU`. CIM has no `Processor Queue Length` field; the raw `Perfmon:System` `Processor Queue Length` timechart with `where queue_len > 4` is the authoritative SLO. Use this CIM line only as a parallel CPU pressure signal on the same span and `by host`.
knownFalsePositives: Bursty work can spike queue with modest average CPU. Sustained queue over core count matters. The `>4` thread queue in the main SPL is *not* the same as `>80%` CPU here; tune both in tandem.
